content:
# 1 Corinthians Chapter 11 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in 1 Corinthians Chapter 11 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 11:1 — "Be imitators of me" vs. "Become imitators of me" vs. "you should imitate me"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Become imitators of me, just as I also [am] of Christos" | Literal ginesthe with "become" |
| LOGOS | "Be imitators of me, as I am of Christ" | Standard imperative |
| PNEUMA | "And you should imitate me, just as I imitate Christ" | Dynamic; verb instead of noun construction |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET's "become" is a legitimate translation of ginesthe but overemphasizes process. PNEUMA's shift from "imitators" (noun) to "imitate" (verb) is natural but loses the Pauline noun construction.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 11:2 — "commend" / "praise" / "glad"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I praise you" | Literal epaino |
| LOGOS | "I commend you" | Slightly more formal register |
| PNEUMA | "I am so glad that you always keep me in your thoughts" | Full paraphrase |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "commend" (LOGOS). PNEUMA's "so glad" and "always keep me in your thoughts" is a substantial expansion and tonal shift. EMET's "praise" is acceptable but "commend" better captures the sense of approval for faithful behavior.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 11:3 — "head of a wife is her husband" vs. "head of [the] woman [is] the man" vs. "head of woman is man"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "[the] head of [the] woman [is] the man" | Literal gyne/aner rendered as "woman"/"man" |
| LOGOS | "the head of a wife is her husband" | Renders gyne as "wife" and aner as "husband" |
| PNEUMA | "The head of every man is Christ, the head of woman is man, and the head of Christ is God" | Renders as "woman"/"man" (generic) |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "wife"/"husband" for consistency in the marital context of the head-covering discussion. The Greek gyne and aner can mean "woman/man" or "wife/husband" depending on context. In this passage about head coverings in married worship practice, the marital sense is primary. SOPHIA alternates between "wife" and "woman" in the chapter depending on the immediate context (vv.3, 5, 6, 10, 13 use "wife"; vv.7-9, 11-12 use "woman").

**Status:** Resolved with a context-dependent approach. LOGOS's "wife" is adopted in marital contexts; EMET/PNEUMA's "woman" in creation-order contexts.

---

## 11:4 — "with his head covered" / "having [something] down from [the] head" / "covers his head"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "having [something] down from [the] head" | Hyper-literal kata kephales echon |
| LOGOS | "with his head covered" | Standard interpretation |
| PNEUMA | "covers his head while praying or prophesying" | Combines the condition and action |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET's phrase is transparent to the Greek but opaque in English. The Greek idiom means "having something hanging down from the head," i.e., having the head covered.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 11:5 — "shaven" / "shaved" / "shaving her head"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "having been shaved" | Passive participle |
| LOGOS | "as if her head were shaven" | Archaic form "shaven" |
| PNEUMA | "this is the same as shaving her head" | Active gerund form |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. Minor morphological variant; "shaven" preserves a slightly more elevated register appropriate to the passage.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor variation.

---

## 11:6 — "cover her head" / "cover herself" / "wear a covering"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "let her cover herself" | Literal katakalyptestho — middle voice, "cover herself" |
| LOGOS | "let her cover her head" | Adds "head" for clarity |
| PNEUMA | "she should wear a covering" | Makes the covering a garment |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "cover her head." EMET's reflexive "cover herself" is grammatically faithful but ambiguous in English. PNEUMA's "wear a covering" makes the covering into a specific garment, which may over-specify.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 11:10 — "a symbol of authority on her head" / "authority upon the head" / "a covering on her head to show she is under authority"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the woman ought to have authority upon the head, on account of the angels" | Literal exousia — "authority" without specifying direction |
| LOGOS | "a wife ought to have a symbol of authority on her head, because of the angels" | Adds "symbol of" to clarify |
| PNEUMA | "a woman should wear a covering on her head to show she is under authority" | Interprets exousia as being "under" authority |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering with "a symbol of authority." This is a deeply contested verse. EMET's literal rendering preserves the ambiguity — exousia is "authority," not "a sign of subjection." PNEUMA's "under authority" imposes a specific directional interpretation that the Greek does not require (exousia is the woman's own authority). LOGOS's "symbol of authority" is a compromise that acknowledges the head covering as representing authority without specifying whose authority.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The ambiguity of exousia is partially preserved.

---

## 11:16 — "practice" / "custom" / "custom"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "we have no such custom" | Renders synetheia as "custom" |
| LOGOS | "we have no such practice" | "Practice" — slightly different nuance |
| PNEUMA | "we have no other custom than this" | "No other custom than this" reverses the logic |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "practice." The difference between "practice" and "custom" is minimal. PNEUMA's phrasing "no other custom than this" subtly changes the meaning — the Greek says "we have no such practice" (i.e., the practice of being contentious), not "we have no other practice than head-covering."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's reversal of the referent is noted.

---

## 11:17 — "commend" / "praise" / "praise"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I do not praise [you]" | Literal epaino as "praise" |
| LOGOS | "I do not commend you" | "Commend" for consistency with v.2 |
| PNEUMA | "I cannot praise you" | "Cannot" adds inability rather than refusal |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "commend" (LOGOS) for consistency with v.2. PNEUMA's "cannot" slightly changes the tone from choice to inability.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 11:19 — "genuine" / "approved [ones]" / "God's approval"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the approved [ones] may become evident among you" | Literal dokimoi |
| LOGOS | "those who are genuine among you may be recognized" | "Genuine" for dokimoi |
| PNEUMA | "you who have God's approval will be recognized" | Adds "God's" to specify the source of approval |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "genuine" (LOGOS). PNEUMA's "God's approval" makes explicit what is implied but not stated. "Genuine" captures the testing/proving connotation of dokimoi without over-specifying.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 11:22 — "church of God" / "assembly of God" / "God's church"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the assembly of God" | Renders ekklesia as "assembly" |
| LOGOS | "the church of God" | Standard English rendering |
| PNEUMA | "God's church" | Possessive reordering |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the church of God" (LOGOS). EMET's "assembly" is etymologically accurate but in modern English "church" is the established rendering of ekklesia in Pauline contexts. PNEUMA's reordering is stylistic only.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 11:23 — "betrayed" / "delivered up" / "betrayed"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "in the night in which he was delivered up" | Literal paredideto — "delivered/handed over" |
| LOGOS | "on the night when he was betrayed" | Standard rendering "betrayed" |
| PNEUMA | "On the night when he was betrayed" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "betrayed" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). EMET's "delivered up" is etymologically broader (paradidomi can mean "hand over" in a neutral sense), but in this context the tradition specifically refers to Judas's betrayal, and "betrayed" is the established English rendering.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 11:24 — "which is for you" vs. "which is given for you" vs. "which is broken for you"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the [one] for you" — follows Sinaiticus omitting "broken" | Sinaiticus shorter reading |
| LOGOS | "which is for you" | Same Sinaiticus reading |
| PNEUMA | "which is given for you" | Adds "given" — interpretive addition |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "which is for you." This follows the Sinaiticus reading which omits klomenon ("broken"). PNEUMA's "given" is a reasonable interpretive addition but does not follow the manuscript.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Sinaiticus shorter reading preserved.

---

## 11:25 — "after supper" / "after the supping" / "after supper"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "after the supping" | Hyper-literal meta to deipnesai |
| LOGOS | "after supper" | Standard English |
| PNEUMA | "he took the cup of wine after supper" | Adds "of wine" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "after supper." EMET's "the supping" is archaic. PNEUMA adds "of wine" which, while accurate, is not in the text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 11:25 — Covenant wording expansion by PNEUMA

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "This cup is the new covenant in the blood of me" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "This cup is the new covenant in my blood" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "This cup is the new covenant between God and his people — an agreement confirmed with my blood" | Major expansion with explanatory gloss |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's expansion ("between God and his people — an agreement confirmed with my blood") is a substantial interpretive gloss that, however theologically accurate, should not appear in the translation text. Jesus's words in the institution narrative are sacred tradition and should be rendered with minimal expansion.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Institution narratives require maximal fidelity.

---

## 11:27 — "in an unworthy manner" / "unworthily" / "unworthily"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "unworthily" | Literal anaxios |
| LOGOS | "in an unworthy manner" | Expanded adverbial phrase |
| PNEUMA | "unworthily is guilty of sinning against" | Adds "sinning" to "guilty" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "in an unworthy manner." This clarifies that the adverb modifies the manner of eating, not the person's worth. PNEUMA's addition of "sinning" is interpretive.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 11:29 — "discerning the body" vs. "honoring the body of Christ"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "not discerning the body" | Literal diakrinon to soma |
| LOGOS | "without discerning the body" | Same meaning, smoother syntax |
| PNEUMA | "without honoring the body of Christ" | Changes "discerning" to "honoring"; adds "of Christ" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "without discerning the body." PNEUMA's "honoring" is a legitimate interpretation of what "discerning" entails but changes the verb meaning. Adding "of Christ" specifies what Paul intentionally leaves ambiguous — "the body" (to soma) refers simultaneously to the eucharistic body and the church-body.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The deliberate ambiguity of "the body" is preserved.

---

## 11:30 — "have died" / "have fallen asleep" / "have even died"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a number have fallen asleep" | Literal koimontai — euphemism "sleep" for death |
| LOGOS | "some have died" | Translates the euphemism directly |
| PNEUMA | "some have even died" | Adds "even" for emphasis |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "some have died" (LOGOS). While "fallen asleep" is the literal Christian euphemism, "have died" communicates the severity Paul intends. PNEUMA's "even" adds emphasis not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA interpretive expansion | 8 | Added words to institution narrative, added "of Christ," "of wine," "God's approval," etc. |
| gyne/aner translation (woman/wife) | 1 | Context-dependent rendering strategy adopted |
| Theological term precision | 4 | "Discerning" vs. "honoring," "authority" vs. "under authority," "commend" vs. "praise" |
| EMET literalism | 6 | "The supping," "delivered up," "assembly," hyper-literal constructions |
| Institution narrative fidelity | 2 | PNEUMA's expansions of Jesus's words noted and rejected |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 11 contains two of the most theologically sensitive passages in the NT — the head-covering discussion and the institution narrative. PNEUMA's expansions were most notable (and most problematic) in the eucharistic words of Jesus (v.25), where the consensus demands maximal fidelity to the tradition. The head-covering passage produced the most significant three-way divergence at v.10 (the meaning of exousia), where all three translators imposed different directional readings on an inherently ambiguous Greek term. LOGOS's compromise ("symbol of authority") was adopted as the least interpretively constrained option.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
